abstract:
This paper introduces a high-performance sentiment analysis system designed to categorize the polarity of short, informal texts, such as tweets and SMS messages. Our approach addresses two distinct challenges: determining the overall sentiment of a message (message-level task) and identifying the sentiment of specific words or phrases within a message (term-level task). Built upon a supervised statistical classification framework, the system integrates a diverse suite of surface-form, semantic, and sentiment-based features. A core innovation of this work is the development of several high-coverage, tweet-specific sentiment lexicons. These lexicons were automatically compiled from large-scale corpora using sentiment-related hashtags and emoticons as proxies for labels. Additionally, we generated a specialized lexicon to better capture the nuances of sentiment in negated contexts.

The system achieved first place in the SemEval-2013 "Sentiment Analysis in Twitter" competition, recording F-scores of 69.02 for the message-level task and 88.93 for the term-level task. Subsequent refinements further increased these benchmarks to 70.45 and 89.50, respectively. Beyond the competition data, the system demonstrates state-of-the-art results on both an SMS test set and a corpus of movie reviews. Ablation studies highlight the impact of our automatically generated lexicons, showing that they contribute performance gains of up to 6.5 absolute F-score points.
